content: Hi! I’m a concurrent graduate/undergraduate student at Brown University, with research interests in reinforcement learning, robotics, distributed RL and planning. I currently work in the Intelligent Robot Lab at Brown University, led by Professor George Konidaris. For a resume, please click here.
summary: Hi! I’m a concurrent graduate/undergraduate student at Brown University, with research interests in reinforcement learning, robotics, distributed RL and planning. I currently work in the Intelligent Robot Lab at Brown University, led by Professor George Konidaris.
title: Neev Parikh

authors: Cameron Allen, Neev Parikh, George Konidaris
summary: We introduce a novel set of conditions and prove that they are sufficient for learning a Markov abstract state representation. We then describe a practical training procedure that combines inverse model estimation and temporal contrastive learning to learn an abstraction that approximately satisfies these conditions.
title: Learning Markov State Abstractions for Deep Reinforcement Learning

authors: Kavosh Asadi, Neev Parikh, Ron Parr, George Konidaris, Michael Littman
summary: We introduce deep radial-basis value functions (RBVFs): value functions learned using a deep network with a radial-basis function (RBF) output layer.
title: Deep Radial-Basis Value Functions for Continuous Control

authors: Neev Parikh*, Zachary Horvitz*, Naveen Srinivasan*, Aansh Shah, George Konidaris
summary: We leverage graph-encoded object priors to capture this property and improve the performance of reinforcement learning agents across multiple tasks. We introduce a novel, flexible architecture that utilizes graph convolutional networks (GCNs), which provide a natural method to combine relational information over connected nodes.
title: Graph Embedding Priors for Multi-task Deep Reinforcement Learning

authors: Max Merlin, Neev Parikh, Eric Rosen, George Konidaris
summary: We introduce a novel structured formulation, the *Locally Observable Markov Decision Process*, which assumes that partial observability stems from limited sensor range---objects outside sensor range are unobserved, but become fully observed once they are within sensor range.
title: Locally Observable Markov Decision Process
